Facts.text: Treaty of Andelot: King Guntram of Burgundy names cousin Childebert II as heir
Albanian George Kastriotis Skanderbeg and his forces liberate Kruja in Middle Albania from the Ottomans and raise the Albanian flag.
Duke of Alva forces Bishop of Haarlem Nicolaas van Nieuwland to resign due to the clergyman's alcoholism
Natchez Indians massacre 138 French men, 35 French women, and 56 children at Fort Rosalie near the site of modern-day Natchez, Mississippi
French troops and Indian forces attack Saratoga, NY, killing many and taking prisoners
Britain condemns Convention of Kloster-Zeven
Second Continental Congress formally establishes the Continental Navy
The Treaty of Hopewell is signed between the Confederation Congress of the United States of America and the Cherokee people
US pays $800,000 and a frigate as tribute to Algiers and Tunis
Cossacks occupy Utrecht
The Times of London is first printed by automatic, steam-powered presses built by German inventors Friedrich Koenig and Andreas Friedrich Bauer, making newspapers available to a mass audience
Panama declares independence from Spain
Ka Lahui: Hawaiian Independence Day - The Kingdom of Hawaii is officially recognized by the United Kingdom and France as an independent nation.
Church of San Francisco dei Minori Conventuali in Bologna, Italy initiated with premier of Rossini's Tantum ergo
Olympia forms as capital of Washington Territory
Dutch army stops Chinese uprising in Borneo
Confederate congress officially admits Missouri to Confederacy
Battle at Cane Hill, Arkansas (475 casualties)
Battle of Hooly Spring, MS
3rd day of Battles at Waynesboro/Jones's Plantation, Georgia
US Civil War: Battle of New Creek, West Virginia (Rosser's Raid, Ft Kelly)
Ku Klux Klan trials began in Federal District Court in South Carolina
British explorer Verney Cameron reaches East Africa
Battle at Lydenburg South Africa: Gen Wolseley beats Sekhukhenes Pedi-Zulu
Germany defeats Hottentotten in Warmbad SW-Africa
154 men die in coal mine explosion at Marianna, Pennsylvania
World War I: Following a war-induced closure in July, the New York Stock Exchange re-opens for bond trading.
1st German air attack on London
Sigmund Romberg's revue "Over the Top" premieres in NYC
Bucovina voted for the union with the Kingdom of Romania
Kilmichael Ambush, Battle of the Irish War of Independence.
6 ex-ministers executed in Greece
RAF Captain Cyril Turner gives the first skywriting exhibition in NYC, spelling out "Hello USA. Call Vanderbilt 7200," 47,000 people call
Pieter Jelle Troelstra leaves Dutch 2nd Chamber
Grand Ole Opry premieres as WSM Barn Dance on WSM Radio in Nashville, Tennessee
J McHugh & D Fields' musical "Delmar's Revels" premieres in NYC
Chicago fullback Ernie Nevers sets NFL record for most points scored in a single game with all 40 in the Cardinals' 40–6 rout of Chicago Bears; Nevers has NFL record 6 touchdowns and 4 extra points
Spanish comic playwright Pedro Muñoz Seca comments "I am starting to believe you are not intending to count me among your friends!" before being executed by the Republican army
4th Heisman Trophy Award: Davey O'Brien, Texas Christian (QB)
Soviet government revokes Russian-Finnish non-aggression treaty
Dutch law professor Rudolph Cleveringa arrested by Nazis
German troops vacate Rostov
492 die in a fire that destroys Cocoanut Grove nightclub, fueled by flammable tropical decor, in Boston, Massachusetts
1st allied ship sails into Schelde Antwerp
400 Rotterdammers attack coal warehouse
Detroit Tigers pitcher Hal Newhouser is named AL MVP
In reprisal 40 Dutch men are executed by Nazis
US 121st Infantry regiment occupies Hurtgen
Australian Services draw second Victory Test Cricket v India at Calcutta
Dutch Nazi Anton Mussert sentenced to death
French Fourth Republic government led by Georges Bidault resigns
Edited film serial "Hopalong Cassidy" premieres on TV, later becoming the 1st network western series on NBC
Walter O'Malley fires Burt Shotton as Dodgers manager
Military coup under Col Adib el-Shishakli in Syria
1st pro football game in The Netherlands
KCKT (now KSNC) TV channel 2 in Great Bend, KS (NBC) 1st broadcast
1956 NFL Draft: Gary Glick from University of Colorado A&M first pick by Pittsburgh Steelers
KMVI (now WMAU) TV channel 12 in Wailuku, HI (IND) begins broadcasting
KTHV TV channel 11 in Little Rock, AR (CBS) begins broadcasting
Lee Calhoun leads an American trifecta in the men's 110m hurdles at the Melbourne Olympics; hurdles Olympic record 13.5s to beat teammates Jack Davis and Joel Shankle
Leonid Spirin leads a Soviet Union 1-2-3 to win the men's 20 kilometre walk gold medal in 1:31:27.4 at the Melbourne Olympics; beats teammates Antanas Mikėnas and Bruno Junk
Photography begins on French film "Et Dieu... créa la femme / And God Created Woman"
Shirley Strickland de la Hunty of Australia retains her 80m hurdles Olympic title in OR 10.7s beating German Gisela Birkemeyer by just 0.2s at the Melbourne Games
Soviet runner Vladimir Kuts wraps up the Olympic middle distance double by winning the 5,000m gold medal at the Melbourne Games; runs Olympic records in both 5k and 10,000m events
AL announces Opening Day in 1959 will be earliest ever, April 9
Chad becomes an autonomous republic within French community
Congo & Mauritania become autonomous members of French Community
George "Punch" Imlach becomes coach of NHL's Toronto Maple Leafs
KCOO (now KABY) TV channel 9 in Aberdeen, SD (ABC) begins broadcasting
Legendary cricket fast bowler Wes Hall makes his Test debut for West Indies v India in 1st Test at Bombay; Hall's match figures 4/107
US reports 1st full-range firing of an Intercontinental ballistic missile
KOMC (now KSNK) TV channel 8 in McCook - Oberlin, NB (NBC) begins
CBS radio expands hourly news coverage from 5 to 10 minutes
Mauritania gains independence from France (National Day)
General Meeting of UN debates New Guinea
Telegraph between Netherlands & Indonesia restored
Crusher beats Verne Gagne in St Paul, to become NWA champ
The Beatles "She Loves You" unusually, returns to #1 in UK record chart and reaches 1 million copies sold
WHNT TV channel 19 in Huntsville, AL (CBS) begins broadcasting
1965 NFL Draft: Tucker Frederickson from Auburn University first pick by New York Giants
France performs underground nuclear test at Ecker Algeria
Mariner 4 launched; 1st probe to fly by Mars
Browns' Leroy Kelly sets club record for most punt return yds (109)
Coup in Burundi overthrows monarchy; a republic is declared
Dominican Republic adopts constitution
33rd Heisman Trophy Award: Gary Beban, UCLA (QB)
Infielder Ted Sizemore becomes 7th Dodger to win NL Rookie of Year
2 members of the IRA are killed in a premature bomb explosion in the Bogside area of Derry
Arab League summit in Algiers recognizes Palestine
Baltimore Oriole Al Bumbry wins AL Rookie of Year

John Lennon's last concert appearance, as a guest of Elton John, Madison Square Garden, New York City NYC; they perform "Whatever Gets You Thru the Night"; "Lucy In The Sky With Diamonds"; and "I Saw Her Standing There"
"As the World Turns" and "The Edge of Night", the final two American soap operas that had resisted going to pre-taped broadcasts, air their last live episodes.
Democratic Republic of East-Timor proclaimed
Test Cricket debut of Michael Anthony Holding, WI v Australia Brisbane
Wings release single "Venus & Mars/Rock Show" medley
44th Heisman Trophy Award: Billy Sims, Oklahoma (RB)
Air New Zealand DC-10 crashes into Mt Erebus on Antarctica, killing 257 people, making it New Zealand's deadliest peacetime disaster
LA Dodger Rick Sutcliffe wins NL Rookie of Year
Bear Bryant wins his 315th game to out distance Almos Alonzo Stagg, becomes college football's winningest coach
9th NASA Space Shuttle Mission: Columbia 6 launches
6th Belgium government of Martens forms
Hilbert van der Duim skates 1 hour world record 39.4928 km
OPEC reaches oil production accord
South African Airways Boeing 747 crashes into Indian Ocean, 159 die
Carlos Reina wins Honduras presidential election
Norway votes against joining the European Union
James Brady, former white house press secretary, suffers a heart attack
Final episode of "Beavis & Butt-head" on MTV
First public appearance of the Kosovo Liberation Army, an ethnic Albanian guerrilla group that fought for the independence of Kosovo from Serbia
Albanians overwhelmingly endorse a new constitution in a national referendum
Ukrainian politician Oleksander Moroz begins the Cassette Scandal by publicly accusing President Leonid Kuchma of involvement in the murder of journalist Georgiy Gongadze
Male Poʻo-uli dies of Avian malaria in the Maui Bird Conservation Center in Olinda, Hawaii before it could breed, making the species in all probability extinct
American Congressman Duke Cunningham admits to accepting $2.4 million in bribes and pleads guilty to tax evasion and conspiracy to commit bribery
Sweden technically enters the recession after experiencing contraction of 0.1% in the second and third quarter
54 people are killed and 120 are injured by two car bombs in Damascus, Syria
Plane carrying Brazilian Chapecoense football team crashes near Medellín, Colombia killing 71 players and journalists
India's Supreme Court rejects appeal to block global release of controversial film "Padmavati"
Australian state Queensland raises its fire warning to "catastrophic" for the first time as 130 fires burn
French director Luc Besson accused of sexual harassment by five more women, adding to four already made public
European parliament declares a climate emergency
Iraqi security forces open fire on protesters killing at least 25 in Nasiriya a day after the Iranian embassy in Najais is burnt down
Mosconi Cup nine-ball pool, Las Vegas, Nevada: US retains trophy with an 11-8 win over Europe; MVP: Skyler Woodward (USA)
Zimbabwe on the brink of man-made starvation according to the UN
At least 110 people killed in attack on Koshobe village in north-east Nigeria by Boko Haram jihadist group
Thousands of farmers begin entering Delhi to protest proposed agriculture reforms
Dan Campbellrecords his first victory as head coach of the Detroit Lions with a win against the Minnesota Vikings in Week 13
Honduras presidential elections: Xiomara Castro elected country's first female president (declared 2 Dec)
50 million birds killed in record-breaking outbreak of avian flu across the US according to Department of Agriculture, amid similar outbreaks elsewhere around the world
Canadian scientists announce discovery of two new minerals elaliite and elkinstantonite, never seen on earth before from 'Nightfall' meteorite found in Somalia
Cocaine “super-cartel” that controlled one-third of European trade broken up in 'Operation Desert Light' by Europol, with 30 tones of the drug seized and 49 people arrested
London's Horniman Museum, becomes first UK museum to return items, including Benin Bronzes to Nigeria, originally looted from Benin City by British troops in 1897
Merriam-Webster's word of the year is 'gaslighting' while Collins' is 'permacrisis'
Qatar Football World Cup Chief Hassan Al-Thawadi confirms 400-5000 immigrant workers died during the building of world cup venues
Recent protests in Iran have killed 451 protesters and 60 security forces according to Human Rights Activist in Iran group, with 18,000 detained. Iranian government says number killed is 300.
41 Indian construction workers successfully rescued after 41 days trapped in a collapsed tunnel in Uttarakhand
Archaeologists reveal the first evidence, of two different early human species coexisting in the same place, Homo Erectus and Paranthropus boisei in footprints in Kenya
Australia is the first country to pass legislation banning children under 16-years-old from social media
More than 1 million Ukrainian households are left without power after a widespread Russian missile strike targets the country's energy infrastructure
Eleanor of Castile, wife of KingEdward Iof England, dies in Northamptonshire. Crosses are erected where her body rests on the way to London.
Portuguese navigatorFerdinand Magellanbegins crossing the Pacific Ocean
Lecture by SirChristopher Wren, Professor of Astronomy, at England's Gresham College inspires formation of scientific group that later becomes the Royal Society of London
Pierre Corneille's comedic play "Tite et Berenice" premieres in Paris
Blackbeardattacks and captures a French merchant slave ship, which he renames as his flagship the "Queen Anne's Revenge"
Anne BonnyandMary Readare tried, found guilty of pirating, and sentenced to death in Spanish Town, Jamaica, although their discovered pregnancies win them stays of execution
Jean-Baptiste Le Rond d'Alembertis elected to the French Academy of Sciences
Second Boer War: Battle of Modder River (Cape Colony), British Lord Methuen defeats Boer forces of Piet Cronjé andKoos de la Rey
Gustav Mahlerconducts premiere of his 4th Symphony in G, at Kaim-Saal concert hall in Maxvorstadt, Munich, Germany to mixed reviews
Arthur Griffithforms Sinn Féin in Dublin
CanadianTommy Burnsretains his world heavyweight boxing title after being pushed to a 20-round draw byPhiladelphia Jack O'Brienin Los Angeles, California
In Haverhill, Massachusetts, scrap-metal dealerLouis B. Mayeropens his first movie theater

Sergei Rachmaninoff's Piano Concerto No. 3, one of the genres most difficult, premieres at the New Theatre in New York City, with the composer as soloist and the New York Symphony Society conducted by Walter Damrosch
Emiliano Zapataproclaims the Plan of Ayala Mexico
Ismail Qemalideclares Albania independent  in Vlorë of the Ottoman Empire after 400 hundred years of rule
KaiserWilhelm IIof Prussia & Germany abdicates
American-born LadyNancy Astorelected as the first female member of the British House of Commons (to take her seat)
7th French government ofAristide Briandsworn-in
NHL goalieGeorges Vezinacollapses and dies 4 months later of TB
Richard E. Byrdmakes his 1st South Pole flight
Howard Hanson's 2nd Symphony "Romantic" premieres
Groucho Marxperforms on radio for the first time
A Dallas grand jury delivers a murder indictment againstBonnie ParkerandClyde Barrowfor the January 1933 killing of Tarrant County Deputy Malcolm Davis
Winston Churchilltells British Prime MinisterStanley Baldwinnot to under estimate German air power
Dmitri Shostakovich's Suite for Jazz Orchestra No. 2 premieres in Moscow, USSR
Hans Frank, Nazi Governor-General of Poland, organizes Judenrat
Franklin D. Roosevelt,Winston ChurchillandJoseph Stalinmeet at the Tehran Conference in Iran to map out strategy
John Van Druten's "I am a Camera" premieres in NYC
"Look Homeward, Angel" play based on the book byThomas Wolfeadapted by Ketti Frings and starringAnthony Perkinspremieres in NYC
Milwaukee Braves' future HOF pitcherWarren Spahnwins Cy Young Award
PopeJohn XXIIIpublishes encyclical Princeps Pastorum
German writerMartin Walser's play "Der Abstecher" (The Detour) premieres in Munich
First radio pulsars detected by British postgraduateJocelyn Bell Burnelland her supervisor Antony Hewish at Cambridge University
John Lennonis fined £150 for unauthorized drug possession
LA Dodgers tradeFrank Robinsonto California Angels
Baseball CommissionerBowie Kuhnsuspends NY Yankees ownerGeorge Steinbrennerfor two years because of his Federal conviction for illegal contributions to political campaigns
Bobby Orrplays his last game for the Boston Bruins
Atlantic Records releases "Briefcase Full of Blues", the debut album by The Blues Brothers (Dan AykroydandJohn Belushi); album tops the chart and becomes best-selling blues record of all-time
Cincinnati Reds fire their managerSparky Andersonafter 9 years
PopeJohn Paul II's first papal visit to Turkey, almost 1 1/2 years before Turkish native Mehmet Ali Agca attempts to kill him
New Zealand general election won by ruling National party and Prime MinisterRobert Muldoon
Germany'sSylvia Hanikastages remarkable comeback to upset tennis iconMartina Navratilova1–6, 6–3, 6–4 and win her only WTA Tour Championship in NYC
Over 250 years after their deaths,William Pennand his wife Hannah Callowhill Penn are made Honorary Citizens of the United States
USReaganadministration exceeds SALT II arms limitations for 1st time
Picasso's "Acrobat & Harlequin" sells for $38.46 million
Queen Latifahreleases her debut hip hop album "All Hail the Queen" featuring single "Ladies First"
Rickey Hendersonsigns record $3,000,000 contract per year with baseball's Oakland Athletics
Conservative Party choosesJohn Majorto succeedMargaret Thatcheras leader and Prime Minister of the United Kingdom
Singapore'sLee Kuan Yewresigns, ending his term as Singapore's longest-serving Prime Minister
Convicted serial killerJeffrey Dahmeris clubbed to death by fellow inmate Christopher Scarver in the Columbia Correctional Institution gymnasium in Portage, Wisconsin
In an all-American final,Pete SamprasbeatsAndre Agassi6–1, 7–5, 6–4 to win his 5th and final ATP Tour World Championship tennis title in Hanover, Germany
Ballon d'Or: FC Barcelona's Brazilian midfielderRonaldinhois named best football player in Europe ahead of Chelsea midfielder Frank Lampard and Liverpool's Steven Gerrard
In a classic matchup,Roger Federerwins his 5th season-ending ATP World Tour Finals tennis title with a 6–3, 3–6, 6–1 victory overRafael Nadalof Spain in the final in London, England
"The Hobbit: An Unexpected Journey" 1st of the Hobbit film series, directed byPeter Jackson, starringMartin FreemanandIan McKellen, premieres in Wellington, New Zealand

British boxerTyson Furybeats UkrainianWladimir Klitschkoby unanimous decision to win WBA, WBO, IBF, IBO, The Ring magazine and lineal heavyweight titles in Düsseldorf, Germany; ends Klitschko's 9 year reign as champion
Jamie Vardyscores for Leicester City in a 1-1 draw against Manchester United at the King Power Stadium; EPL record 11th consecutive game in which Vardy scores
Musical "Hamilton" starringLin-Manuel MirandaandAnthony Ramossets new record for most money earned in a week on Broadway - $3.3 million
Uhuru Kenyattasworn in for a second term as president of Kenya
Joe Bideninjures his foot playing with his dog Major
Batu Khan's Mongol army lays siege to Kyiv and begins assaulting its city walls with catapults, city falls 8 days later

Whistler v. Ruskin, the most famous trial in art history, ends with artist James McNeill Whistler awarded a token farthing in compensation after suing the writer and critic John Ruskin for libel, seeking £1,000 damages
Women vote in a national election for the first time, in the New Zealand general election
America's first auto race is organized by the "Chicago Times-Herald" from Chicago to Evanston and back; six cars participate in the 55-mile race, and Frank Duryea wins, averaging 7 mph
Manuel I Komnenos, Emperor of the Byzantine Empire (1143-80), born in Constantinople (d. 1180)
Margaret Tudor, Queen consort of Scots as wife of James IV of Scotland (1503-13), born in Westminster Palace, London (d. 1541)
James Whitelocke, English judge and politician under James I and Charles I (House of Commons 1610-22), born in England, likely London (d. 1632)
Hans Nansen, Danish statesman, born in Flensburg (d. 1667)
Francesco Sforza Pallavicino, Italian theologist and historian (Istoria del Concilio di Trento), born in Rome, Papal States (d. 1667)
Jean-Baptiste Lully, Italian-born French composer (Miserere Suites de Symphonies et Trios), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1687)
Willem de Vlamingh, Dutch sea captain who explored the then "New Holland", the central west coast of Australia, born in Oost-Vlieland, Dutch Republic (d. c. 1698)
Jan Palfijn, Flemish physician and inventor (obstetrical forceps), born in Kortrijk, Flanders (d. 1730)
Edward Hyde, 3rd Earl of Clarendon, British nobleman, supporter of William III, Governor of New York and New Jersey (1701-08), born in England (d. 1723)
Jean Cavalier, French Huguenot chief of the Camisards, born in Ribaute, France (d. 1740)
Nathaniel Bliss, English 4th Astronomer Royal (1762-64), born in Bisley, Gloucestershire, England (d. 1764)
Sophie Magdalene of Brandenburg-Kulmbach, Queen consort of Denmark and Norway (1730-46), born in Castle Schonberg, Bavaria (d. 1770)
Jacob Mossel, Dutch administrator (Governor-General of the Dutch East Indies, 1750-61), born in Enkhuizen, Dutch Republic (d. 1761)
Matthäus Fischer, German organist and composer, born in Ried, Swabia (d. 1840)
Princess Louise of Orange-Nassau, daughter of Stadtholder William V Prince of Orange and Wilhelmina of Prussia, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1819)
Luke Howard, British manufacturing chemist and meteorologist (nomenclature system for clouds), born in London (d. 1864)
Augsut Kestner, German art collector and diplomat, born in Hanover, Germany (d. 1853)
Ferdinand Ries, German pianist, composer, and Beethoven's secretary and copyist, baptized in Bonn, Electorate of Cologne, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1838)
Victor de Broglie, 3rd Duke of Broglie, 14th Prime Minister of France (1835-36), born in Paris (d. 1870)
Victor Cousin, French philosopher and educational reformer, born in Paris (d. 1867)
Carl Jonas Love Almquist, Swedish composer (Tornrosens Buck), born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1866)
William Froude, English engineer and naval architect, born in Devon, England (d. 1879)
Maximilian II of Bavaria, King of Bavaria (1848-64), born in Munich (d. 1864)
Ludwig Mathias Lindeman, Norwegian organist, composer, musical folklorist, born in Trondheim, Norway (d. 1887)
Lawrence O'Bryan Branch, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Enfield, North Carolina (d. 1862)
E. M. Grace, English cricket all-rounder (1 Test, HS 36; Gloucestershire CCC), born in Bristol, England (d. 1911)
Helen Magill White, American educator and 1st woman to earn a Ph.D. in the U.S., born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 1944)
Gottlieb Haberlandt, Austrian botanist, born in Mosonmagyaróvár (d. 1945)
Alexander Kastalsky, Russian composer, born in Moscow, Russian Empire (d. 1926)
Alfonso XII, King of Spain (1874-85), born in Royal Palace of Madrid, Madrid (d. 1885)
William Stanley, American inventor of the induction coil (electricity transformer), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1916)[1]
Carel Adolph Lion Cachet, Dutch lithographer and wood carver, born in Amsterdam (d. 1945)
James Allen, English writer (As a Man Thinketh), born in Leicester, England (d. 1912)
Lindley M. Garrison, American lawyer and 46th U.S. Secretary of War (1913-16), born in Camden, New Jersey (d. 1932)
Dick Lilley, English cricket wicket-keeper (35 Tests from 1896 to 1909), born in Holloway Head, Warwickshire, England (d. 1929)
František Drdla, Czech composer, born in Žďár nad Sázavou, Czech Republic (d. 1944)
William H. Lewis, American College football center and coach (first African-American selected as an All-American; Harvard), born in Berkley, Virginia (d. 1949)
Bert Vogler, South African cricket all-rounder (15 Tests, 64 wickets, BB 7/94, 2 x 50; Natal, Transvaal; MCC), born in Swartwater, South Africa (d. 1946)
Jozef van Mierlo, Belgian Jesuit and literary (Hadewych), born in Turnhout, Belgium (d. 1958)
Alexander Block, Russian poet (Dvenatsat), born in St. Petersburg, Russia (d. 1921)
Marinus van Meel, Dutch aviator and aircraft manufacturer, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1958)
Stefan Zweig, Austrian novelist (Beware of Pity), playwright (Jeremiah), and memoirist (The World of Yesterday), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1942)
Paul Hastings Allen, American composer, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1952)
Bobby Watson, American comedian (Hitler Gang, Boys Town), born in Springfield, Illinois (d. 1965)
Ernst Röhm, German army officer and chief organizer of Adolf Hitler’s Storm Troopers (Sturmabteilung, or SA; Brownshirts), born in Munich (d. 1934)
Jacobo Palm, Dutch Antillean classical organist, violist, pianist, and composer, born in Curaçao, Dutch Antilles (d. 1982)
Gregorio Perfecto, Filipino jurist (48th Associate Justice of the Philippine Supreme Court) and politician, born in Mandurriao, Iloilo, Captaincy General of the Philippines (d. 1949)
Thomas Wood, English composer, born in Chorley, Lancashire, England (d. 1950)
José Iturbi, Spanish concert pianist and conductor who appeared in nearly a dozen 1940s MGM musicals, born in Valencia, Spain (d. 1980)
Dawn Powell, American writer (Turn, Magic Wheel), born in Mount Gilead, Ohio (d. 1965)
Lilia Skala, Austrian actress (House of Games, Flashdance, Charly), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 1994)
Uno Chiyo, Japanese writer (Confessions of Love), born in Iwakuni (d. 1996)
James Eastland, American politician (Sen-D-Miss 1943-68), born in Doddsville, Mississippi (d. 1986)
Nancy Mitford, English author (Love in a Cold Climate), one of the Mitford sisters, born in London (d. 1973)
Henry Picard, American golfer (Masters 1938, PGA 1939), born in Plymouth, Massachusetts (d. 1997)
Alberto Moravia [Pincherle], Italian author (the Indifferent Ones), born in Rome, Italy (d. 1990)
Arturo Frondizi, 35th President Argentina (1958-62), born in Paso de los Libres, Corrientes, Argentina (d. 1995)
Claude Lévi-Strauss, French social anthropologist and ethnologist (structuralism), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 2009)
Roberto Lupi, Italian composer, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1971)
Aleksandar Ranković, Yugoslavic partisan and 1st Vice President of Yugoslavia (1963-66), born in Draževac, Serbia (d. 1983)
David Miller, American film director (Billy the Kid), born in Paterson, New Jersey (d. 1992)
Lotta Hitschmanova, Canadian humanitarian (founder of the Unitarian Service Committee of Canada), born in Prague, Bohemia (d. 1990)[1]
Václav Renč, Czech poet, dramatist and translator, born in Vodochody, Czech Republic (d. 1973)
Heinz Galinski, President (Central council for Jews in Germany), born in Marienburg, West Prussia, Germany (d. 1992)
Morris Louis, American painter (post painterly abstraction), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1962)
Hugo Pos, Surinamese-Dutch poet and writer, born in Paramaribo, Suriname (d. 2000)
Gertrude Jeannette, African American taxi driver, playwright and film and stage actress (Shaft, Cotton Comes to Harlem), born in Urbana, Arkansas (d. 2018)
Evald Okas, Estonian painter, born in Talinn, Estonia (d. 2011)
Pamela Harrison, English pianist and composer, born in Orpington, England (d. 1990)
Lilian Baels, Princess of Réthy, 2nd wife of Belgian King Leopold III, born in London, England (d. 2002)
Kees Schilperoort, Dutch radio and TV host, born in Apeldoorn, Gelderland, Netherlands (d. 1999)
Cecilia Colledge, British figure skater (World C'ship gold ladies singles 1937; Olympic silver 1936; first female to land a two rotation jump), born in London, England (d. 2008)
Helen Copley, American newspaper publisher (The San Diego Union-Tribune), born in Cedar Rapids, Iowa (d. 2004)
Gloria Grahame [Hallward], American actress (Crossfire; Sudden Fear; Rich Man, Poor Man), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1981)
Helen Delich Bentley, American politician (Rep-R-MD, 1985-95), born in Ruth, Nevada (d. 2016)
Lorna Beal (née Larter), Australian cricket wicket-keeper (7 Tests; 16 dismissals), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 2020)
Phyllis Jenkins, American literary & theatrical agent community activist, born in New York (d. 2004)
(Gerrit Cornelis) "Kees" Otten, Dutch flautist and recorder player, born in Amsterdam, The Netherlands (d. 2008)
Aurelio de la Vega, Cuban-American composer (Adiós; Variación del Recuerdo), educator (California State University, 1959-92), and poet, born in Havana, Cuba (d. 2022)
József Bozsik, Hungarian footballer (Budapest Honvéd), born in Kispest, Hungary (d. 1978)
Virginia Hewitt, American actress (Carol-Space Patrol), born in Shreveport, Louisiana (d. 1986)
Chuck Mitchell, American actor (General Hospital, Porky's, Better Off Dead), born in Connecticut, United States (d. 1992)
Arthur Melvin Okun, American economist (Misery Index), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1980)
Edward LeBone Molotlegi, Kgosi (king) of the Bafokeng (1956-95), born in  Otsetswe, Botswana (d. 1995)
Jan Fotek, Polish composer (Musica Cromatica), born in Czerwińsk on the Vistula, Poland (d. 2015)
Clarence Fountain, American Grammy Award-winning gospel singer (The Blind Boys of Alabama), born in Tyler, Alabama (d. 2018)
Dervla Murphy, Irish bicyclist and travel author (Full Tilt: Ireland to India with a Bicycle; In Ethiopia with a Mule), born in Lismore, Ireland (d. 2022)[1]
Rinus Ferdinandusse, Dutch journalist, editor (Vrij Nederland, 1969-96), and author (Dovemansoren; Naked Over the Fence), born in Goes, Netherlands (d. 2022)
Thomas Enders, German business executive and diplomat, born in Bruchertseifen, Germany
Ethel Ennis, American jazz singer (If Women Ruled The World), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 2019)
Gato Barbieri, Latin jazz musician (Last Tango in Paris), born in Rosario, Argentina (d. 2016)
Terence Frisby, English actor and playwright (There's a Girl in My Soup), born in London, England (d. 2020)
Hope Lange, American actress (Peyton Place, The Ghost & Mrs Muir), born in Redding, Connecticut (d. 2003)
Joe Knollenberg, American politician (Rep-R-Michigan 1993-2009), born in Mattoon, Illinois (d. 2018)
Carlos Fariñas, Cuban avant-garde composer, born in Cienfuegos, Cuba (d. 2002)
Prince Hitachi, Japanese royalty (sixth born child of Emperor Shōwa and Empress Kōjun), born in Tokyo Imperial Palace, Tokyo
Randolph Stow, Australian author and poet (Suburbs of Hell), born in Geraldton, Western Australia (d. 2010)
Gary Hart, American politician (Sen-D-Co, 1975-86), born in Ottawa, Kansas
Roy McCurdy, American jazz drummer (Sonny Rollins; Cannonball Adderly; Chuck Mangione), born in Rochester, New York
Trisutji Kamal, Indonesian classical pianist and composer (Prayer of Redemption), born in Jakarta, Indonesia (d. 2021)
Richie Roberts, American criminal defense attorney portrayed in "American Gangster" and convicted thief, born in the Bronx, New York
Gary Troxel, American rocker (The Fleetwoods), born in Centralia, Washington
Bruce Channel [McMeans], American singer ("Hey! Baby"), born in Jacksonville, Texas
